Heat-injury integral: two equivalent parameterisations explained

Author

Daniel W.A. Noble (clarification dialogue with Claude)

Published

May 7, 2026

Question

In ms.qmd:166, the heat-injury (HI) integral is now written in two forms — @eq-hi (matching @orsted_suzukii_2024’s exact published form using \(\alpha\) and \(-1/z\)) and @eq-hi-tdt (the canonical TDT form using \(z\) and \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\)). What is the difference between them, and why both?

Short answer

They are the same equation written in two parameterisations. The TDT line \(\log_{10} LT_{50}(T) = \text{slope}\cdot T + \text{intercept}\) is fully determined by two parameters, but you can label it by any two reference points. The two forms in the manuscript correspond to two different choices of reference points; the underlying line, the underlying \(LT_{50}(T)\), and the underlying HI integral are identical.

The TDT line and its parameters

Both forms describe the same straight line in \((T, \log_{10} LT_{50})\) space:

\[ \log_{10} LT_{50}(T) \;=\; \text{slope}\cdot T \;+\; \text{intercept}. \]

This line has two free parameters. Any pair of independent constraints on the line — e.g. a y-intercept and a slope, or two \((T, LT_{50})\) points — fixes it uniquely. Different parameterisations differ in which two numbers you read off, not in the line itself.

Form 1 — @eq-hi: \((\alpha, z)\), anchored at \(T = 0\)

@orsted_suzukii_2024, @jorgensen_drosophila_2019, and @li2023 all parameterise the line by its slope and y-intercept extrapolated to \(T = 0\) °C:

  • \(\alpha\) = \(\log_{10} LT_{50}\) at \(T = 0\) °C — the y-intercept that an OLS regression returns directly.
  • \(-1/z\) = the slope; equivalently \(z\) is the temperature change (°C) producing a 10-fold change in \(LT_{50}\).

So \(\log_{10} LT_{50}(T) = \alpha - T/z\), and the HI integrand is

\[ \frac{1}{LT_{50}(T(\tau))} \;=\; \frac{1}{10^{\,\alpha\,-\,T(\tau)/z}} \;=\; \frac{1}{10^{\,(-1/z)\,T(\tau)\,+\,\alpha}}, \]

giving @eq-hi. Pros: the parameters are exactly what an OLS fit (or the joint Bayesian 4PL’s marginal regression) produces, with no further algebra. Cons: \(\alpha\) is the temperature-zero extrapolation of the line — it has no biological interpretation (no organism is asked how long it survives at 0 °C). It’s a parameterisation convenience, not a phenotype.

Form 2 — @eq-hi-tdt: \((z, CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}, t_{\text{ref}})\), anchored at a biologically meaningful point

@jorgensen_drosophila_2019, @jorgensen_unifying_2021, @orsted_finding_2022, and our manuscript anchor the line at a chosen \((T, LT_{50})\) pair: the temperature at which \(LT_{50}\) equals a chosen reference duration \(t_{\text{ref}}\).

  • \(t_{\text{ref}}\) = chosen reference exposure duration (typically 60 min).
  • \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\) = the temperature satisfying \(LT_{50}(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}) = t_{\text{ref}}\). For \(t_{\text{ref}} = 60\) min this is \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) — “the temperature that kills half the population in 1 hour.”
  • \(z\) = same thermal sensitivity as in Form 1.

These three numbers carry only two degrees of freedom (you choose \(t_{\text{ref}}\), then \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\) and \(z\) are estimated), so the line is still fully specified.

The HI integrand becomes

\[ \frac{1}{LT_{50}(T(\tau))} \;=\; \frac{1}{t_{\text{ref}} \cdot 10^{(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\,-\,T(\tau))/z}} \;=\; \frac{1}{t_{\text{ref}}}\cdot 10^{\,(T(\tau)\,-\,CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}})/z}, \]

giving @eq-hi-tdt. Pros: the parameters are biologically interpretable — \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) is a phenotype experimentalists already think about. Cons: requires picking \(t_{\text{ref}}\) as a convention; not directly returned by an OLS fit.

Why they’re the same equation: explicit algebra

The TDT line connects the two forms via the constraint that the line passes through both \((0, \alpha)\) and \((CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}, \log_{10} t_{\text{ref}})\). Setting \(T = CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\) in the line equation,

\[ \log_{10} t_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \alpha \;-\; \frac{CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}}{z} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \alpha \;=\; \log_{10} t_{\text{ref}} \;+\; \frac{CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}}{z}. \tag{1}\]

Substituting @eq-translation into @eq-hi recovers @eq-hi-tdt algebraically (and vice versa). The HI integrals computed under either form, on the same temperature trajectory \(T(\tau)\), return identical numerical values.

A small numerical sanity check: \(z = 4\) °C, \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}} = 40\) °C, \(t_{\text{ref}} = 60\) min. Then \(\alpha = \log_{10}(60) + 40/4 = 1.778 + 10 = 11.778\). At \(T = 36\) °C, both forms give \(LT_{50} = 10^{11.778 - 36/4} = 10^{2.778} \approx 600\) min, as expected from the \(z = 4\) scaling: 4 °C below \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), \(LT_{50}\) is \(10\times\) longer than \(t_{\text{ref}}\).

When to use which

Situation Preferred form
Implementing the HI integral algorithmically from a regression’s output @eq-hi \((\alpha, z)\) — directly read off slope and intercept
Reproducing a published Ørsted-, Li-, or Rezende-group calculation @eq-hi (matches their exact form)
Communicating to a biological reader what the parameters mean @eq-hi-tdt \((z, CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}})\) — both are recognisable phenotypes
Connecting HI to a \(CT_{max}\) value derived elsewhere in the manuscript @eq-hi-tdt (the manuscript’s \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) derivation feeds straight in)
Cross-walking to the joint Bayesian 4PL output Either — under the joint fit, \(\alpha\) and \(z\) are both linear functions of \(\beta_0\), \(\beta_1\), \(\bar T\) from @eq-linpred plus the asymptote correction in @eq-lt50-corrected

Implications for the manuscript

  • Both forms in the same section is appropriate. The first form lets readers cross-walk to the published TDT/HI literature; the second form connects to the rest of the paper, which uses \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) throughout.
  • The current text frames @eq-hi-tdt as a re-expression of @eq-hi (“equivalently, in canonical TDT form, ?@eq-hi can be re-expressed in terms of \(z\) and \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\)…”). That’s correct and reads naturally — keep it.
  • Joint Bayesian 4PL recovery. Under the joint fit, \(\alpha\) corresponds to \(\beta_0 - \beta_1 \bar T\) plus the asymptote correction term in @eq-lt50-corrected. The supplement at supplement.qmd:90 already defines this “uncentred TDT intercept” \(\alpha\) explicitly. Worth a single sentence in the manuscript noting the recovery, but the supplement carries the algebra.
  • Don’t introduce a third parameterisation. Slope/intercept-on-centred-temperature (\(\beta_0, \beta_1\) with \(T\) centred at \(\bar T\)) is a third way to write the same line, used by @eq-linpred. It’s correct but redundant with \((\alpha, z)\) and would add notational clutter to the HI section. Keep \((\alpha, z)\) as the literature-canonical pair in @eq-hi.

Follow-up: \(t_{\text{ref}}\) as a time-unit converter (does not change \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\))

A separate clarification that came up: \(t_{\text{ref}}\) in the canonical-form equation has two roles, and they are easy to conflate.

Role 1 — defines \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\). When you say \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), you have committed to 1 hour as the reference duration. This is fixed by the experiment and does not change during analysis.

Role 2 — bridges the time unit of integration. The prefactor \(1/t_{\text{ref}}\) in @eq-hi-tdt carries units (1/time), and so does \(di\). Their product is dimensionless, so the integral is well-defined regardless of whether you tick \(i\) in hours, minutes, or seconds — provided \(t_{\text{ref}}\) is expressed in the same time unit as \(i\).

So with \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) already fixed, you can integrate in whichever unit you want by setting \(t_{\text{ref}}\) accordingly:

Time unit of \(i\) \(t_{\text{ref}}\) Prefactor \(100/t_{\text{ref}}\) Static-at-\(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\)-for-1-h check
hours \(1\) h \(100\) \(100 \cdot \int_0^1 1\, di = 100\) ✓
minutes \(60\) min \(\approx 1.67\) \((100/60)\cdot \int_0^{60} 1\, di = 100\) ✓
seconds \(3600\) s \(\approx 0.0278\) \((100/3600)\cdot \int_0^{3600} 1\, di = 100\) ✓

All three return identical \(HI\) for the same temperature trajectory; \(z\) and \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) are untouched. The choice is purely about what time unit you want to tick the clock in.

Practical implication for the manuscript. The current @eq-hi in ms.qmd drops \(t_{\text{ref}}\) because it commits to hours throughout, matching typical hourly field-temperature records. If a future analysis ever needs minute-resolution (e.g. minute-by-minute logger data, as in @orsted_suzukii_2024’s NicheMapR interpolation), \(t_{\text{ref}} = 60\) min can be reinstated in the prefactor without re-fitting anything — same \(z\), same \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), same biological calibration.

Conceptual summary. \(t_{\text{ref}}\) is doing two things at once in the general form: (i) labelling the line via \(CT_{max,t_{\text{ref}}}\) — fixed by the experiment; (ii) carrying the time-unit of the integration — chosen by the analyst. Once you commit to one time unit (hours, in our case), Role 2 collapses to the trivial \(t_{\text{ref}} = 1\) h, and the prefactor disappears from the equation.

Numerical demonstration: the two forms give identical HI

The algebra in @eq-translation says the two parameterisations describe the same line, so the HI predictions from a temperature trajectory must be numerically identical (up to floating-point noise). The following R simulation makes this concrete.

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# --- Synthetic hourly temperature: 5 days, sinusoidal diurnal cycle -----
hours    <- 0:(24 * 5 - 1)
T_series <- 32 + 8 * sin(2 * pi * (hours - 6) / 24)   # range 24–40 °C, peaks at noon

# --- TDT parameters for a hypothetical organism ---------------------------
z         <- 4    # thermal sensitivity (°C per 10-fold change in LT50)
CT_max_1h <- 38   # 1-hour static critical limit (°C)
T_c       <- 28   # critical temperature below which no injury accrues

# --- Form A (Ørsted, α, −1/z), time in hours ----------------------------
# log10(LT50)(T) = α − T/z, with α calibrated so LT50(CT_max_1h) = 1 h:
# 0 = α − CT_max_1h/z  ⇒  α = CT_max_1h/z
alpha_hr <- CT_max_1h / z
rate_A   <- ifelse(T_series > T_c,
                   100 / 10 ^ (alpha_hr - T_series / z),
                   0)
HI_A     <- cumsum(rate_A * 1)     # Δi = 1 h

# --- Form B (canonical, z and CT_max_1h), time in hours -----------------
rate_B <- ifelse(T_series > T_c,
                 100 * 10 ^ ((T_series - CT_max_1h) / z),
                 0)
HI_B   <- cumsum(rate_B * 1)

# --- Numerical equivalence check ----------------------------------------
max_abs_diff <- max(abs(HI_A - HI_B))
sprintf("Max |HI_A − HI_B| over 5-day series: %.3e", max_abs_diff)
[1] "Max |HI_A − HI_B| over 5-day series: 3.553e-15"

Temperature trajectory (the input shared by both calculations):

ggplot(tibble(hour = hours, T = T_series), aes(hour, T)) +
  geom_line(colour = "darkred") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = CT_max_1h, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = T_c,       linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = max(hours), y = CT_max_1h,
           label = "CTmax,1hr", hjust = 1, vjust = -0.4, size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = max(hours), y = T_c,
           label = "T_c",       hjust = 1, vjust = -0.4, size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Time (h)", y = "Temperature (°C)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

Cumulative HI under both parameterisations, overlaid:

df <- tibble(
  hour                              = hours,
  `Ørsted form (α, z)`              = HI_A,
  `Canonical form (z, CTmax,1hr)`   = HI_B
) |>
  pivot_longer(c(`Ørsted form (α, z)`, `Canonical form (z, CTmax,1hr)`),
               names_to  = "Parameterisation",
               values_to = "HI")

ggplot(df, aes(hour, HI,
               colour    = Parameterisation,
               linetype  = Parameterisation)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.4, alpha = 0.65) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid", "dashed")) +
  labs(x = "Time (h)", y = "Cumulative HI (%)",
       subtitle = sprintf("Max |HI_A − HI_B| = %.2e — numerical noise only",
                          max_abs_diff)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

What the simulation shows

  1. The two HI curves overlap exactly. Visually they cannot be distinguished; the dashed and solid lines lie on top of each other.
  2. The maximum absolute difference between \(HI_A\) and \(HI_B\) is at the level of floating-point noise (≪ 10⁻¹⁰), confirming that the two forms are not just “approximately” equivalent — they are algebraically equivalent.
  3. HI accumulates fastest during the daily peaks when \(T > CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), slows when temperature is between \(T_c\) and \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), and pauses entirely overnight when \(T < T_c\). The shape of the cumulative HI curve is the same under both parameterisations because both use the same TDT line — they just label its slope and intercept differently.
  4. Reusable as a sanity check. Anyone implementing HI from \((α, z)\) output (e.g. an OLS fit of \(\log_{10} LT_{50}\) on \(T\)) and someone implementing from \((z, CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}})\) (e.g. our joint Bayesian 4PL) should agree to numerical precision when given the same temperature trajectory and the consistency relation \(\alpha = CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}/z\) (with time in hours). If they don’t, there is a unit error somewhere.

Follow-up: does the same equivalence hold if we anchor at \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\) instead of \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\)?

Yes — provided the prefactor and time unit of \(i\) are adjusted to match, the equation gives identical HI predictions. The TDT line itself does not move; only its anchor on the line changes.

The key relationship. \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\) and \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) sit on the same TDT line at different points. At \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), \(LT_{50} = 60\) min; at \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\), \(LT_{50} = 1\) min. Solving for both points on \(\log_{10} LT_{50}(T) = \alpha - T/z\) and subtracting gives

\[ CT_{max_{1\text{min}}} \;=\; CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}} \;+\; z \log_{10}(60) \;\approx\; CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}} \;+\; 1.778\,z. \tag{2}\]

For our simulation parameters (\(z = 4\), \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}} = 38\) °C), this gives \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}} \approx 45.1\) °C — about 7 °C hotter than the 1-hour limit, exactly as expected for an organism that can survive 1 minute much higher than it can survive 1 hour.

Three forms must agree. With the conversion above, we now have three formally distinct parameterisations of the same line:

  • Form A (Ørsted): \(\alpha\) and \(-1/z\), time in hours.
  • Form B (canonical, current @eq-hi): \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) and \(z\), time in hours.
  • Form C (canonical, anchored at 1-minute reference): \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\) and \(z\), time in minutes (with prefactor \(100/t_{\text{ref}} = 100/(1 \text{ min}) = 100\)).

The simulation below adds Form C and confirms it agrees with Forms A and B to floating-point precision.

# Form C: canonical with CT_max_1min, integration in minutes
CT_max_1min <- CT_max_1h + z * log10(60)        # ≈ 45.108 °C

# Resample temperature to minute resolution (constant within each hour)
T_series_min <- rep(T_series, each = 60)
above_Tc_min <- T_series_min > T_c

rate_C    <- ifelse(above_Tc_min,
                    100 * 10 ^ ((T_series_min - CT_max_1min) / z),  # %/min
                    0)
HI_C_min  <- cumsum(rate_C * 1)                  # Δi = 1 min

# Sample HI_C at end-of-hour boundaries to align with hourly HI_A and HI_B.
# HI_C_min[60·k] is HI accumulated through the end of hour k, which is what
# cumsum(rate_A * 1)[k] = HI_A[k] also represents — so the alignment is direct.
HI_C <- HI_C_min[seq(60, length(T_series_min), by = 60)]

# Numerical comparison across all three forms
max_abs_diff_AC <- max(abs(HI_A - HI_C))
max_abs_diff_BC <- max(abs(HI_B - HI_C))

sprintf("CT_max_1min derived: %.3f °C", CT_max_1min)
[1] "CT_max_1min derived: 45.113 °C"
sprintf("Max |HI_A − HI_C|: %.3e", max_abs_diff_AC)
[1] "Max |HI_A − HI_C|: 1.355e-10"
sprintf("Max |HI_B − HI_C|: %.3e", max_abs_diff_BC)
[1] "Max |HI_B − HI_C|: 1.355e-10"
df3 <- tibble(
  hour                                            = hours,
  `A: Ørsted (α, z), hours`                       = HI_A,
  `B: canonical (z, CTmax,1hr), hours`            = HI_B,
  `C: canonical (z, CTmax,1min), minutes`         = HI_C
) |>
  pivot_longer(-hour, names_to = "Parameterisation", values_to = "HI")

ggplot(df3, aes(hour, HI,
                colour    = Parameterisation,
                linetype  = Parameterisation)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.4, alpha = 0.55) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("solid", "dashed", "dotted")) +
  labs(x = "Time (h)", y = "Cumulative HI (%)",
       subtitle = sprintf("Max |A − C| = %.2e, Max |B − C| = %.2e",
                          max_abs_diff_AC, max_abs_diff_BC)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.direction = "vertical")

What this confirms

  • The 1-minute and 1-hour anchors describe the same TDT line. \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\) is higher than \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) by \(z \log_{10}(60) \approx 1.78\,z\) °C, exactly as the line geometry requires.
  • HI is reference-invariant. The HI predicted at each hour boundary is bit-for-bit identical (up to floating-point noise) regardless of whether you anchor at \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\), \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\), or write the line in \((\alpha, z)\) form. The choice is a labelling convenience; it never changes what HI value comes out.
  • Watch out for unit consistency. What does not work is plugging \(CT_{max_{1\text{min}}}\) into the equation as currently written in @eq-hi (which has \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) and is integrated in hours) without adjusting either the prefactor or the time unit. The same equation with the wrong \(CT_{max}\) value gives wildly different — and wrong — HI.

Practical implication. If a co-author or reviewer reports a \(CT_{max}\) value at a non-standard reference duration (say, \(CT_{max_{30\text{min}}}\)), don’t substitute it into @eq-hi directly. Either convert it to \(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\) via ?@eq-ctmax-conversion-style algebra (\(CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}} = CT_{max_{30\text{min}}} - z \log_{10}(2)\)) and then use the equation as written, or rewrite the equation with a matching \(t_{\text{ref}}\) and time unit. The line is one object; how you label its anchor is your choice.

Verification status

  • Algebra (@eq-translation) verified by direct substitution in both directions and cross-checked against the numerical example above.
  • @orsted_suzukii_2024 form: [verified] against the paper’s Methods text on 2026-05-07 — they write the integrand denominator exactly as \(10^{(-1/z)\cdot \max(T_i, T_{i+1}) + \alpha}\), with \(\beta = -1/z\) as slope and \(\alpha\) as y-intercept of \(\log_{10}(Lt_{50}) = \beta T + \alpha\).
  • @li2023 form: [verified] — they use \(\alpha\) for slope and \(\beta\) for intercept (Greek-letter convention reversed from Ørsted), but the underlying \(1/LT_{50}\) structure is identical.
  • @jorgensen_drosophila_2019 form: [verified] — they parameterise the static-to-dynamic conversion (their Eq. 7) using \(z\) and \(sCT_{max}\), equivalent to @eq-hi-tdt.
  • The manuscript’s \(\alpha\)-at-\(T=0\) definition matches the supplement’s supplement.qmd:90 “uncentred TDT intercept” derivation under the asymptote-corrected midpoint regression.
